title: AMD Am386 released March 2, 1991

processed.summary:
• The **Am386** was a pin-compatible clone of the Intel 80386 DX, marking AMD's critical transition from a licensed manufacturer to a direct market competitor.
• It featured a **40MHz clock speed**, which significantly outperformed Intel's flagship 33MHz offering, providing superior performance in the 32-bit era.
• The architecture utilized **static CMOS** technology, which allowed for lower power consumption and higher reliability compared to contemporary designs.
• This release effectively broke the **Intel monopoly** on the x86 instruction set, fundamentally altering the economics of the PC hardware industry.
key_results: AMD successfully delivered a reverse-engineered x86 processor after years of litigation.
The Am386DX-40 provided a faster, cheaper alternative to Intel's high-end processors.
Introduced energy-saving features by allowing the CPU to operate at lower power states.
Facilitated the growth of the 'white box' PC market by providing accessible high-performance chips.
Established the financial and technical foundation for AMD's future custom architectures like the K5 and K6.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: AMD launched the Am386 in 1991, successfully challenging Intel's monopoly and establishing the long-standing competitive x86 processor market.

title: The inner workings of TCP zero-copy

processed.summary:
- The article provides a deep dive into the **Linux kernel's** implementation of **TCP zero-copy**, explaining how it bypasses traditional memory-to-memory copies between kernel and user space.
- It details the architecture of **MSG_ZEROCOPY** and how the kernel tracks data pages from the application buffer until the **NIC** (Network Interface Card) signals that the data has been sent.
- The analysis covers the performance trade-offs, specifically how **TLB shootdowns** and page table manipulations can introduce overhead that competes with the cost of `memcpy` for smaller data sizes.
key_results: Zero-copy is most effective for large payload transfers where CPU cycles spent on copying are the primary bottleneck.
Hardware support is required for receiving zero-copy (RX) via header-data splitting to align payloads to page boundaries.
User-space applications must handle asynchronous notification via an error queue to know when memory can be reused.
Memory alignment is critical; unaligned buffers force the kernel to revert to traditional copying mechanisms.
The cost of virtual memory management (mmap/munmap) often makes zero-copy slower than standard TCP for payloads under 10KB-64KB.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Toke Høiland-Jørgensen explains how TCP zero-copy optimizes high-bandwidth networking by utilizing page mapping to eliminate redundant CPU memory copies.

title: Big Breakfast Alters Appetite, Gut Health

- Impact analysis involves monitoring **gut microbiota diversity**, satiety hormone signaling (GLP-1, PYY), and metabolic markers throughout the trial period.
key_results: Large breakfasts significantly increased daily satiety scores compared to smaller morning meals.
Higher morning protein intake correlated with improved hunger management during weight loss.
The study observed distinct shifts in gut microbial composition and short-chain fatty acid production.
